title: The Fourteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## September 15, 2019 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fourteenth Sunday after Pentecost in the Church calendar, placing the sermon within Ordinary Time (the long stretch between Pentecost and Advent). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this Sunday were Exodus 14:19-31 (the crossing of the Red Sea), Psalm 114, Romans 10:8b-13, and Matthew 18:21-35 (Peter's question about forgiveness and the parable of the unforgiving servant). The emphasis on radical forgiveness would have been central to any sermon preached this day.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating significant internal tensions in 2019 regarding sexuality and marriage, with General Convention (held biennially) not meeting until July 2018, so the church was in the post-convention period. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Konda Mason (elected 2015), was actively engaged in racial justice initiatives and urban ministry work. No major diocesan crises or bishop elections were occurring at this specific moment.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing unseasonably warm fall weather in mid-September 2019. The city was focused on local elections scheduled for November 2019, with mayor's races and ballot measures generating civic discussion. Saint John's Cathedral, located in downtown Denver on the edge of Capitol Hill, was serving a diverse urban congregation amid discussions about gentrification and affordable housing in the neighborhood.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was in its third year, with the House impeachment inquiry into Ukraine dealings beginning in earnest this same week. Hurricane Dorian had recently devastated the Bahamas and was moving up the eastern seaboard (September 3-8), dominating news coverage. The 2020 Democratic primary race was intensifying with the fourth debate scheduled for late September. The U.S. economy was showing signs of stress, with the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates for the second time in a month.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Hong Kong pro-democracy protests were escalating into their fourth month of confrontation with police and government crackdowns, drawing international attention. The Amazon rainforest fires in Brazil were at peak visibility in global media coverage, sparking climate activism conversations. Brexit negotiations remained unresolved in the UK as Boris Johnson pursued a hard-line approach toward the October 31 deadline.

CULTURAL MOMENT

America in September 2019 carried a sense of institutional strain and fragmentation. The national political temperature was rising toward impeachment. Climate anxiety was becoming more visible in mainstream conversation following the Amazon fires. The Denver congregation would have been aware of local tensions around growth, gentrification, and changing identity in their Capitol Hill neighborhood. There was a general mood of uncertainty about institutional stability, making themes of grace, forgiveness, and faith countercultural offerings in the pulpit.
